does: This paper introduces Controlled Behavioral Divergence (CBD), an API-only black-box unlearning framework designed to remove the influence of sensitive, copyrighted, harmful, or outdated data from large language models without access to model parameters or internal activations. CBD operates by using two auxiliary models to create controlled behavioral divergence between inputs that should be retained and those to be unlearned, computing an unlearning relevance score from this divergence, and routing target prompts away from the model being unlearned. The method addresses the challenge of preserving utility on retained data even when it shares similar semantic patterns with unlearning targets, enabling practical data removal for edge-deployed LLMs accessed only through APIs.
connects: This work builds on the emerging field of machine unlearning, which has grown in response to privacy regulations and concerns about unwanted training data influence in neural networks. Prior research on unlearning has typically assumed white-box access to model weights and gradients, but CBD extends these ideas to the API-only black-box setting increasingly common in practice. The work also connects to research on prompt routing and behavioral steering of language models, as well as studies on the challenge of removing data influence when retained and unlearned data are semantically similar. It responds to practical limitations of existing unlearning approaches that struggle with computational efficiency and utility retention in production settings where models are deployed behind APIs.
enables: This framework enables several follow-up directions and practical capabilities. It allows edge devices and downstream users of API-based LLMs to request data removal without requiring model developers to retrain systems, supporting privacy rights and regulatory compliance. The behavioral divergence approach could be extended to more sophisticated auxiliary model architectures or ensemble methods for improved discrimination between retained and unlearned behaviors. Future work might explore how CBD scales to large-scale unlearning scenarios with many disjoint data removal requests, integrate it with active learning to selectively collect edge data, or develop theoretical guarantees on the completeness of unlearning. The method also opens possibilities for real-time unlearning pipelines in production systems where prompt classification happens at inference time.
